result:
At 1:03 a.m., the switchboard clicks like a jar of beetles under a warm hand. The desert is quiet enough to hear the fluorescent tubes sing, quiet enough that the ventilation fan recites a mantra you stop noticing around midnight and then notice again at two. Beyond the window the two-lane highway holds a single set of headlights that appear, pass, and are gone like an idea you almost had. Coyotes yip in the wash and then fall back into their stitched silence. Out here, the air is dry enough to make paper feel sharp, as if you could cut a finger on a song title.

Mara sits in the dim control room with the battered foam on the mic brushing her mouth. The board’s faders are rubbed bare silver at their favorite volumes. The little red bulb that says ON AIR burns a steady, patient red—no hurry, no agitation, just the simple fact of transmission. She can feel the station hum through the desk, through the heels of her hands on the Formica, like a faraway engine that never quite arrives.

“Good morning to whoever’s awake,” she says, halfway into a window of dead hours that everyone else calls night. “You’re tuned to KLVN, serving three counties of dust and devotion. I have a stack of records and a thermos of coffee, and I’m taking your dedications if you have anybody you love and can’t tell.”

The first call is always the trucker who claims he’s hauling feathers. He’s been making that joke so long it’s more like a greeting. Sometimes he’s up near the state line, sometimes he says he can see the refinery torches breathing through a dust of moths. He always asks for the same song that sounds like a train cresting something it’s not sure about, and Mara always says, “You got it,” and pulls the record from where its sleeve is barely holding.

The second call is often the nurse on her fourth night, consonants velcroed together by fatigue. She tells Mara the number of beds they have empty and the number of crickets that come to the back door when the sun is just a rumor again. The other night she said, “Sometimes I believe if you whisper into the oxygen line, the whole building hears you,” and Mara played a song that felt like breathing.

There are oddities that she notes in a stenographer’s spiral notebook she keeps tucked under the console like a relic. Nights when the ionosphere hangs like a bell and solves its strange math with whatever they fling into it. Skip, Hank told her once, when he trained her. His Polaroid is thumbtacked to the bulletin board in the hallway, face ghostly from time, the word Hank penned on the bottom border in a cheer someone else probably wrote. He was muttering skip the night she first saw the board go feral—stations hundreds of miles apart stepping on each other like neighbors yelling through thin walls.

On skip nights, a teenager called from Aguirre Park to say he’d tuned the same song on two different radios and they’d drifted apart like fish in a current. On skip nights, someone in a town with a grain elevator she could see out the studio window phoned to tell her about a parade she knew had been canceled last year. A woman called to ask Mara to play the song they’d danced to on the day his papers came through, even though his papers are sitting folded under a magnet on the woman’s refrigerator right now and he is asleep in the room down the hall. Mara has learned to nod at these knots in the thread, to keep her scissors away from them.

Between the songs, there’s absolute space. You could pitch a tent there. You could lay down your whole life and let it air out side by side with everybody else’s. When the light on Line Two starts blinking with its polite insistence, Mara punches it in and says, “KLVN, I’m here,” and means it.

“You don’t know me,” says a voice that is a rustle and a whisper, a library breath. “But you do.”

“I do?” Mara asks, smiling without meaning to.

“You will,” the voice says, and sighs, as if even being on the line is relief. “It’s raining here. The creosote smell, you know?” It is the smell that pretends the desert is forgiving. “I’m making a list of things I thought would ruin me that did not.”

Mara glances at the barren station window, at the stars stitched into the sky like tiny glass buttons. “Will you read me some?”

“Sure,” the voice says, and does, and for the span of a gentle guitar line Mara can hold both the future’s rain and the present’s dust without either slipping.

Some nights the station eats itself. The aging transmitter rustles up a noise like someone walking on old film. The turntable belt complains. In a drawer, there is an archaeology of adapters and connectors, and a jar of screws that came out of somewhere and never went back. Mara could build a whole bird from the metals in this room. The bird would rattle when it flew.

Once, months ago, a boy called to say he could see Jupiter from the bleachers and how could anything with that many storms not get tired. He had a telescope he’d bought from a yard sale, and he was patient; he told Mara he waited for the wobble of our atmosphere to calm down and the planet would snap into a certainty that made him swear without meaning to. Mara told him about the first time she saw a satellite snag a sunbeam at dawn and how it made her feel both chosen and overlooked, and he said, “That is exactly it,” and the line went soft and then quiet. A week later, the school burned in a lightning fire that turned the sky the color of old pennies until it rained ash on everything they owned. And now, on certain skip nights, she swears she hears a voice at the edge of the music asking whether the math test is still on Friday, like a tide that hasn’t read later tides yet.

She does not correct the voices that arrive from angles she cannot quite name. She picks the songs that make a person feel like the night is not all posture and wolves. She reads the announcements for lost dogs and found keys and bake sales rescheduled due to wind. She says birthdays on air like she’s handing them back to their owners. She thinks, sometimes, that frequencies were never meant to be loyal to chronology; they care only about finding the path of least resistance.

At 3:19 a.m., the phone rings again. A man with a wind-up clock in his voice. He says, “You played that song on my wedding.” He names a date Mara has not yet inhabited. “I drove all night because my sister said if I got to the top of the ridge by dawn I’d see the valley do that thing with the light, and she was right, and I named my boy after the road we took. It was a good day. Can you play the song again?”

“Of course,” Mara says, and flips through the stack until her fingers find what must be found. The song’s first chord opens like a door that has never been locked. The air in the room changes. It’s not magic, or if it is, it’s the kind that hides in ordinary places where you have to be looking a long time.

When the sun finally tries the horizon, the studio ghosts fall back into whatever corners they prefer. Morning DJs with big voices and better posture will come in smelling like toothpaste and weather. The small red light will rest. Before she leaves, Mara checks the mailbox at the front of the low block building because people still mail their dedications sometimes, a stamp like a bruise on the return address. Today there is a postcard, the photograph on the front sun-bleached to a general blue. The postmark is a year she remembers because she was small enough to sleep on the backseat on long drives without thinking of bones. The handwriting is a cousin to her own. Thank you for last night, it says. We felt less alone.

She stands with the postcard in her two hands, as if it could be weighed against anything. The highway is already counting cars. Far off, somewhere, a song comes up through another person’s speaker, through a kitchen where coffee is heating, through a truck cab where a hand drums half a beat behind. She thinks: the job is not to know. The job is to keep the channel clear, to lift the gate, to lay a little light down on the road so someone you don’t know can steer.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
